Were You Asked This?
Design a Proximity Alert System for Apple Tags

Design a proximity alert system that monitors Apple Tags and notifies users when a tag moves outside a predefined range from the user's location.

Asked at:

Google

An Apple Tag proximity alert system is a mobile-first service that keeps your personal items within reach: it monitors the distance between your phone and your registered tag(s) and notifies you when a tag moves outside a user-defined range. Think of features like "Notify When Left Behind" on AirTag or Tile, combining Bluetooth, crowdsourced sightings, and push notifications to provide timely alerts.

Interviewers ask this to see how you handle real-time location streams, mobile constraints (battery, background execution), noisy signals (GPS/Wi‑Fi/BLE), and large-scale event processing. Strong answers balance on-device and backend computation, specify alerting semantics that avoid false positives, and show a scalable, fault-tolerant pipeline from telemetry ingestion to notification delivery.

Community Solutions
Design a Proximity Alert System for Apple Tags
by Shantam Garg
2
7
Design a Proximity Alert System for Apple Tags
by SmallHarlequinMarsupial210
0
4
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to pair and register tags to their account and manage ownership.

Users should be able to set a proximity threshold (e.g., near, medium, far) and notification preferences.

Users should receive real-time alerts when a tag moves beyond the defined proximity from their phone.

Users should be able to view the current or last known location and status (in-range, out-of-range, last seen time) of each tag.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you compute separation and trigger alerts with low latency while avoiding false positives from noisy location signals?

How do you handle intermittent connectivity for both the phone and the tag while keeping alerts meaningful?

How would you scale ingestion of location heartbeats and perform proximity checks for millions of tags and users?

How will you deliver alerts reliably to mobile devices under OS background constraints and still keep the UI live in foreground?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Proximity status and alerts must transition in near real time from telemetry to user notification. You need push-based delivery (WebSockets/APNs), event-driven processors, and client state synchronization to keep latency low.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Phones and tags emit frequent heartbeats. The system must ingest and process high write throughput, dedupe, and compute proximity continuously without hotspots or global scans.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Alerting is a workflow: detect threshold breach, debounce/hysteresis, notify, await ack, and possibly escalate. Modeling this as a multi-step process makes it reliable and auditable.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, partitioned event log for telemetry ingestion (phone and tag sightings), enabling stateful stream processing, replay for bug fixes, ordered handling per tagId, and smooth backpressure under bursts.

Read More
Redis

Redis is ideal for ephemeral proximity state with TTL, fast counters, and GEO operations for quick distance checks. It also supports lightweight pub/sub for in-app real-time updates and rate limiting to protect downstream systems.

Read More
DynamoDB

DynamoDB fits the durable, high-availability storage for user accounts, tag registry/ownership, alert preferences, and short alert history. Its predictable throughput and TTLs align well with at-scale mobile backends.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Uber

Both systems process continuous location updates, compute proximity between two entities (driver–rider vs. phone–tag), and push near real-time updates, requiring partitioned ingestion and fast geospatial checks.

Read
Practice
Strava

Telemetry ingestion from mobile devices, noisy GPS data smoothing, and time-windowed computations are central to both designs, even if the user-facing outcomes differ (activity tracking vs. alerts).

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Low-latency fanout and real-time session updates to mobile clients mirror the alert delivery path, including background/foreground handling, deduplication, and connection management.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

No debouncing or hysteresis on proximity thresholds

Treating any single noisy sample as truth will spam users with flapping alerts and erode trust. You need time-based windows, confidence scoring, and separate enter/exit thresholds to stabilize decisions.

Centralized, synchronous geofence checks on every update

A single bottleneck or global scan per heartbeat will not scale with millions of tags and leads to high latency. Partition the stream, co-locate state with computation, and avoid cross-partition calls in the hot path.

Ignoring mobile constraints (battery, background execution) and relying only on server-side logic

If the design forces constant high-rate updates or assumes the app is always active, it will drain battery and break under OS limits. Balance on-device filtering/sampling with server processing and use push channels appropriately.
